title: Multi-probe detection of domain nucleation across the metal-insulator transition in VO$_2$
authors: Shubhankar Paul, Giordano Mattoni, Amitava Ghosh, Pooja Kesarwani, Dipak Sahu, Monika Ahlawat, Ashok P, Amit Verma, Vishal Govind Rao, Chanchal Sow
abstract: Electronic and structural degrees of freedom are often intimately coupled in strongly correlated systems, which result in intriguing macroscopic and microscopic phenomena. Using the well-studied material VO$_2$ as a prototype, here we explore the domain distribution across the metal-insulator transition (MIT). We use macroscopic as well as microscopic techniques, such as first-order reversal curve (FORC) and infrared imaging, to probe the domain distributions across the MIT. This study compares MIT in thin films of VO$_2$ with different grain sizes grown by pulsed laser deposition and dc sputtering. We explore the relation between the nature of the FORC distribution and the corresponding thermal hysteresis due to interactions between the supercooled metallic domains and surrounding insulating matrix. Our multi-probe study with quantitative analysis provides a correlation between the growth, domain interaction, and domain nucleation process in MIT.
summary: This paper investigates how the microstructure of VO2 thin films dictates the nature of the metal-insulator transition. By comparing large-grain and small-grain samples, the authors demonstrate that grain size influences domain nucleation and the symmetry of thermal hysteresis. The study provides a quantitative link between growth conditions and the macroscopic hysteretic response.
main_problem: Understanding the microscopic origin of domain nucleation, distribution, and hysteresis during the metal-insulator transition (MIT) in VO2 thin films.
main_result: The study finds that large-grain VO2 exhibits a symmetric, continuous transition, whereas small-grain VO2 shows an asymmetric transition with multiple conduction channels driven by supercooled metallic domains and inter-grain interactions.
method: A multi-probe approach combining electrical resistivity measurements, First-Order Reversal Curve (FORC) analysis, and infrared (IR) thermal imaging.
model_or_system: VO2 thin films grown via two different regimes: P-VO2 (large grains, ~40 nm, via Pulsed Laser Deposition) and S-VO2 (small grains, ~20 nm, via DC magnetron sputtering) on c-plane Al2O3 substrates.
key_observables: Electrical resistivity, thermal hysteresis width (delta T), FORC distribution (peaks and energy barriers), and Insulating Fill Fraction (IFF).
important_parameters: Grain size (20 nm vs 40 nm), transition temperature (T_MIT), and thermal hysteresis width (9 K vs 16 K).
main_assumptions: Emissivity is assumed to be constant within a given sample and phase for IR imaging analysis.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows resistivity vs temperature and temperature derivatives; Figure 2 presents FORC curves and 2D contour plots of distribution; Figure 4 includes 3D histograms of IR intensities.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of domain dynamics in VO2, describes the two growth regimes (P-VO2 and S-VO2), presents transport and structural characterization, details the FORC and IR imaging analysis, and concludes by correlating microstructure with phase transition symmetry and hysteresis.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Resistivity vs temperature curve of (a) P-VO2 and (b) S-VO2 thin film. Inset figure shows the temperature derivative of log(ρ).

Figure 2. The family of cooling FORCs for (a) P-VO2 and (b) S-VO2 films. (c-d) Out of this forty FORCs (shown in grey), four curves 1-4 are selected in color. FORC distribution function is plotted against temperature for these two samples. (e-f) The FORC distribution contour plot with new coordinate (Ta, Tu) as defined in the main text. Blue and red color reflect the reversible and irreversible nature of the phase, respectively.

Figure 3. (a) Photograph of thermal imaging set-up which consisting of IR camera and Peltier stage, (b) sample holder with copper pads, and (c) IR image recorded at room temperature, where the black region on top of the samples are the Ag paint that used to attach the contact wires. Temperature-driven thermal imaging across the MIT of (d) P-VO2 and (e) S-VO2 during heating and cooling. The color scale indicates the raw values of local temperature measured by infrared camera. Due to higher reflectivity, metallic regions appear cooler (darker), while the insulating region, with lower reflectivity, appears warmer (lighter).

Figure 4. Quantitative analysis of IR-imaging across the MIT: (a),(b) histogram of local sample temperatures relative to that of copper, collected from the films during heating from 330 K to 380 K. A uniform distribution for P-VO2 and a multimodal distribution for S-VO2. The insulating fill fractions (IFF) with temperature for (c) P-VO2 and (d) S-VO2.
